# Christiane Reves (German 101)

<!-- vimeo: 1111474974 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

[▶ Watch on Vimeo](https://vimeo.com/1111474974)


Picture this you at a cafe in Berlin, ordering coffee, chatting with locusts totally at ease. Now imagine practicing those moments before you go. No memorizing, just a patient buddy walking you through real conversations. Hi, I'm Tiano Reeves. Until recently, I was the lower division coordinator for German at A SU Arizona State University and this fall I'm starting as language program director for German at NYU. This past year I created language study, a project selected for ASU AI innovation Challenge to explore how chat GPD can help students practice authentic conversations in foreign language As language educators, we know interpersonal communication is at the heart of developing proficiency, but this kind of learning environment is often unavailable outside the classroom. Online students in asynchronous courses, in particular face challenges such as limited speaking partners, reduced contact hours, delayed feedback, mismatched content, and speaking anxiety. And this is where I saw AI's potential to provide always available partners, unlimited speaking time, instant feedback, and tailormade tasks, as well as the private space to, without the fear of judgment, to keep it human centered and ethically grounded. We built language body within as U'S principled innovation values. We created privacy, we kept, kept the data protected and safe. Human agency students and instructors are in control, cultural sensitivity, respectful and inclusive prompts. Access. The tool was available to all our learners. At the first excitement of seeing it work. We built the first full model language body drew from my German 1 0 1 topics, introductions and readings, schedules and hobbies and families and friends, as well as the related grammar and vocabulary. I happened to be teaching a summer 2024 session, so we could put it into action in real course settings right away. Here's a quick look at one of the early sessions. Chat. GPT Voice can now facilitate real-time conversational speech between the person and the ai. An assistant teaching professor of German at a SU has developed a custom GPT called Language Buddy to offer personalized language learning practice anytime, anywhere. The custom GPT offers a series of prompts to facilitate beginner to intermediate conversation practice in German. Hello Benedict. Hello Benedict Hobby, often in New York. Language Buddy provides students with realtime conversations at their level and provides a transcript of the conversation to their professor to provide additional feedback. I started with a very simple prototype, but then we experimented testing different versions, reorganizing the structure, and fine tuning the design. Students really appreciated the availability, the privacy, and the real conversation feel. One of my students, Jackson Woods said, being able to have language buddy when I couldn't meet with other students was really beneficial. It provided context and the ability to speak with somebody in a way that might be like a native speaker or at least somebody on my same skill level. Early bumps like language level mismatch, high speaking speed and limited response accuracy were reported, but the overall feedback was clear. Most of the students said they would use it again once the kinks would be worked out. Hearing that I knew that language but could grow far beyond a single course. I reached out for partners and the a i acceleration team from Ed plus ASU online learning arm jumped in with instructional designers, tech support and resources. I also connected with other colleagues, for example, in French and Russian who were really eager to collaborate. But after countless tweaks of the system level prompt, further improvements in the student experience came when we shifted from just refining the bot to actively training students to use it effectively. We built in clear instructions, offered quick troubleshooting, help and coach them to treat it like a real conversation, explain the target language, pause naturally, and ask for clarification. For example. As a result, students felt more in control and gained a real sense of agency in their learning. For me as an instructor, offering language study brought clear benefits. I had more documented practice from all the recordings and I I could see some feedback in real time. It was faster for me to review student work, but I was also still in control of the content. There were still some is issues and limitations. It was not embedded in the learning platform. It required students to build new skills to use it effectively, and it still faced some accuracy issues. So what are my takeaways? This project showed me that AI can expand learning opportunities. We've proven the concept built on human-centered language expert driven design that puts learners first. The next step is to build on this momentum, integrating it into an LMS, for example, expanding to other languages, conducting formal outcome studies, and deepening the cultural dimension of the experience so that students cannot only connect with the language, but on a deeper level with people, places, and traditions behind it. So for me, this project opened new ways for my students to practice with AI while keeping the human connection central. Next, we need to add authentic visuals, regional dialects, and immersive tools that we are to make language learning richer and culturally embedded. Let's keep the conversation going and shape this future together. Okay?
